title: Switch Hitter

jokes.text: Let him see he's not the only one who builds solid houses, you know?
jokes.setup: Michael explains why he's holding a business meeting at home — to impress a competitor.
jokes.punchline: The irony is immediately undercut by everything that follows (the house is falling apart, the family is dysfunctional), making the boast absurd on arrival.
jokes.explanation: Works as dramatic irony since the audience knows Bluth construction is a scandal. Sets up the episode's running joke about the crumbling house. Rewatch bonus because the joke deepens with knowledge of the Bluth Company's fraudulent building practices.

jokes.text: I think he'll be pretty impressed, knock on wood. [wood collapses/clatters]
jokes.setup: Michael says 'knock on wood' as a superstitious good-luck phrase while hosting a meeting to show off their solid construction.
jokes.punchline: The knocking on wood causes something to immediately break or clatter, literalizing the idiom and proving the house is not solid.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured literalization gag. The idiom 'knock on wood' is flipped into a literal cause of destruction, which simultaneously makes fun of Michael's hubris and the Bluth Company's shoddy construction — two core show themes at once. The economy is exceptional: one phrase does all the work. This is a genuine 8 because it's structurally elegant and layered.

jokes.text: The repeated 'knock on wood' callback — Michael says it again and something falls again
jokes.setup: Michael has already said 'knock on wood' once and caused something to fall.
jokes.punchline: He says it again at 01:54 and again something clatters — the running gag escalates with the second callback.
jokes.explanation: The repetition of the gag is well-timed and escalates properly. The joke gets funnier because it's now a reliable pattern — Michael is oblivious even after the first collapse. Classic Arrested Development running-gag structure.

jokes.text: Ann? Hmm. That'll play well with Sitwell. 'Where's your son?' 'He's out with Ann.' 'Who's Ann?' 'Don't ask me, I'm just the boy's father.'
jokes.setup: Michael mentions George Michael is out with Ann, his girlfriend.
jokes.punchline: Gob does a mock dinner-party routine about how unmemorable Ann is — her invisibility is so total that even her boyfriend's father can't account for her.
jokes.explanation: Part of the long-running 'Her?' Ann-dismissal gag. Gob's theatrical recreation of the social humiliation is perfectly in character — he'd actually perform this at a party. The multi-step dialogue structure (mock Q&A) is more sophisticated than a single punchline and earns the laugh by committing fully.

jokes.text: [Clatters] 'Damn it. This thing got pushed in again.' / 'Gonna need a butter knife.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just been ranting that the Bluth family knows what they're doing and don't need Sitwell's help.
jokes.punchline: Something in their solid Bluth-built home immediately breaks and requires a butter knife to fix — undercutting Gob's confidence perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Second beat of the construction quality irony. Works because it immediately follows Gob's pompous speech. The 'butter knife' detail is a nice specific touch.

jokes.text: George Michael's got these powerful little fingers. He should be here.
jokes.setup: A butter knife is needed to fix a broken appliance vent. Gob complains George Michael isn't there.
jokes.punchline: Gob's characterization of George Michael as having 'powerful little fingers' useful for home repair is bizarre and oddly specific — the phrase is both affectionate and condescending.
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character moment — Gob's obliviousness about how weird this compliment sounds is very on-brand. Not a standout but earns its place.

jokes.text: He said he would help me on this essay for The Old Man and the Sea... You have to read this, and then explain it, and — this is important — say it in my own words. Smart, but not too smart. Let's have a four-syllable max.
jokes.setup: Buster explains he needs help with an essay on 'The Old Man and the Sea.'
jokes.punchline: The escalating specificity — 'say it in my own words,' 'smart but not too smart,' 'four-syllable max' — reveals Buster wants Michael to ghostwrite his homework while pretending it's teaching. The 'four-syllable max' is the perfect peak of absurdity.
jokes.explanation: Near-excellent. The escalation from 'help with an essay' to the precise specification 'four-syllable max' is perfectly calibrated Buster stupidity — he's not dumb, he's just an arrested child who has optimized his neediness. 'Smart, but not too smart' is extremely quotable and character-specific. Genuinely impressive writing.

jokes.text: Oh, here I go! Don't worry. It has not fallen into the garage. Knock on wood. [loud clattering]
jokes.setup: Tobias is attempting to fix the oven vent. He reassures everyone it hasn't fallen.
jokes.punchline: He says 'knock on wood' — the episode's running gag — and immediately causes a collapse. Tobias is now a third person to trigger the gag.
jokes.explanation: The running gag pays off a third time with a new character, which signals it's fully established. 'Oh, here I go!' is wonderfully Tobias — theatrical self-narration before failure. The callback escalates well.

jokes.text: 'Confidence Man Two.' 'Oh, yeah? I didn't see the first Confidence Man.' 'No. Confidence Man Two is my character.'
jokes.setup: Tobias announces he's auditioning for a role in 'Confidence Man Two,' which sounds like a sequel.
jokes.punchline: He clarifies that 'Confidence Man Two' is the character's name, not a sequel — a completely absurd character name that also describes Tobias's actual need to be someone else.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely exceptional misdirection joke. The setup perfectly exploits reasonable audience assumption (sequel), the punchline is absurd but internally logical, and the irony that Tobias — a man who has no confidence and is always playing a role — is auditioning to literally be 'Confidence Man Two' adds thematic resonance. The economy is superb: three lines, fully loaded. This comfortably sits at 8+ because the layering (wordplay + character irony + absurdist logic) is exactly what great sitcom writing looks like.

jokes.text: And besides, don't you have school? No. Um — [chuckles] — Today is 'Help Your Dad Follow His Dream' day.
jokes.setup: Tobias questions whether Maeby should be skipping school to accompany him to an audition.
jokes.punchline: Maeby invents 'Help Your Dad Follow His Dream' day on the spot with complete confidence — a perfect encapsulation of her con-artist character who always has an immediate, deadpan lie ready.
jokes.explanation: Very character-specific to Maeby, the teenage con artist. The invented holiday is both absurd and emotionally barbed (her dad is genuinely dreaming and she's enabling it for her own reasons). Works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: Great. We can take the carpool lane.
jokes.setup: Maeby offers to join Tobias to 'help him prepare,' which he's enthusiastic about.
jokes.punchline: His immediate pivot to the practical benefit of taking the carpool lane is hilariously mundane — he completely misses the emotional warmth of his daughter wanting to spend time with him.
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias obliviousness in miniature. The bathetic pivot to carpool logistics undercuts the father-daughter moment perfectly. Short, punchy, character-true.

jokes.text: The only help we need is from each other. — And apparently whatever's in that bottle.
jokes.setup: Michael makes an earnest speech about family self-sufficiency.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately clocks the Teamocil bottle in Lindsay's hand, undercutting Michael's inspiring speech with a brutally accurate observation.
jokes.explanation: Effective deflation of Michael's sincerity. Gob's aside works because he's right and the timing is perfect. Not exceptional but solid sitcom construction.

jokes.text: Teamocil: the Tobias folk band supplement flashback — 'Teamocil may decrease your sex drive.' 'Good job, buddy.' 'Thanks, friend.'
jokes.setup: Michael recognizes Teamocil as the supplement Tobias and Lindsay were previously selling.
jokes.punchline: The flashback reveals Tobias formed a FOLK BAND to market a dietary supplement, and the band's upbeat harmony on 'Teamocil may decrease your sex drive' followed by the aggressively cheerful 'Good job, buddy / Thanks, friend' is peak Tobias absurdity. The side effect is the selling point.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The concept — a folk duo singing pharmaceutical side effects with relentless positivity — is strikingly original and deeply character-specific to Tobias's theatrical obliviousness about his marriage. 'Good job, buddy / Thanks, friend' as a duet response to decreasing sex drive is inspired: the supplement literally caused their entire problem (killed the marriage's intimacy) and yet they celebrated it as a product. The layers of irony are dense and the joke is still funny in isolation. Comfortably an 8+.

jokes.text: Since I've been taking this again, I've been getting along great with my husband. — You're taking it for the side effect, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Lindsay mentions she's been getting along great with Tobias since taking Teamocil.
jokes.punchline: Michael correctly deduces she's using the sex-drive-suppressing supplement as a marriage aid — she's happy WITH her husband because she no longer wants him.
jokes.explanation: Sharp character comedy. The dark logic (suppressing attraction to your husband makes you like him more) is a genuinely bleak commentary on the Tobias-Lindsay marriage, delivered as a quick joke. Michael's instant, resigned comprehension is perfectly played.

jokes.text: I even tried to convince them he's gay, but no one would believe that a woman like me would have a gay son.
jokes.setup: Lucille explains she tried everything to get Buster out of the army.
jokes.punchline: Her attempted ruse was claiming Buster is gay — which fails because her self-image as a glamorous, virile woman makes a gay son implausible. She's simultaneously insulting herself and demonstrating breathtaking narcissism.
jokes.explanation: Peak Lucille — narcissism so complete it loops back around to self-own. The logic is airtight within her worldview: SHE couldn't produce a gay son, so the lie was unbelievable. Also a darkly funny commentary on how she'd weaponize her son's sexuality as a manipulation tool without a moment's hesitation. High character integration.

jokes.text: Well, you certainly tried. You guys wore matching outfits till he was 12. [Narrator:] Even once on a magazine cover that had been mocked within the family for years.
jokes.setup: Lucille claims no one would believe she has a gay son.
jokes.punchline: Michael notes they wore matching outfits until Buster was 12 — implying the gay-coding was obvious — and the narrator reveals there's a famous (mocked) magazine cover proving it.
jokes.explanation: The magazine cover visual gag (implied, not shown in subtitles) adds a layer. The narrator's dry characterization of a family photo as 'mocked within the family for years' is excellent — this is the show acknowledging the dysfunction has been the source of private shame. Solid layered joke.

jokes.text: Get me a vodka, rocks. — Mom, it's breakfast. — And a piece of toast.
jokes.setup: Michael objects to Lucille ordering a vodka on the rocks at breakfast.
jokes.punchline: Rather than defending or reconsidering, Lucille adds 'and a piece of toast' — as if the toast makes the vodka reasonable. The deadpan escalation of adding a food item as moral cover is perfect Lucille.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. The 'and a piece of toast' as a fig leaf for daytime drinking is a perfect Lucille joke — she's not defending herself, she's just making the order slightly more respectable in her own mind. The structure (objection → doubled-down order + minor concession) is textbook and executed flawlessly. One of the funniest three-line exchanges in the episode.

jokes.text: That hairless freak is coming here? None of his hair is real, you know. — You mean the guy we're meeting with can't even grow his own hair? Come on!
jokes.setup: Lucille disparages Sitwell's alopecia. Gob picks up on it.
jokes.punchline: Gob's outrage ('Come on!') is a perfect blend — he's genuinely outraged on Lucille's behalf while also being absurdly focused on the hair issue as if it's a moral failing. His performative indignation is funnier than the insult itself.
jokes.explanation: A decent character beat for both Lucille and Gob. Gob's 'Come on!' is his signature reaction and it fits, but the joke is fairly broad — mocking alopecia with a 'hairless freak' isn't particularly inventive.

jokes.text: It's called alopecia, and I'd appreciate it if we could all be sensitive to it, okay?
jokes.setup: Lucille and Gob are mocking Sitwell's hair loss.
jokes.punchline: Michael's resigned, parental request for sensitivity — the only adult in a room of children — is funny because it's so futile. He's essentially asking leopards to change their spots.
jokes.explanation: The 'Michael as long-suffering straight man' beat. Works because of his obvious hopelessness. Not exceptional but functional.

jokes.text: Then why is he always waving giant $10 million checks over his head every time some— Go ahead, Mom. Finish your thought. 'Every time some children's hospital needs funding'?
jokes.setup: Lucille begins a rant against Sitwell being 'modest and generous,' framing his charitable giving as a character flaw.
jokes.punchline: Michael sarcastically completes her sentence, revealing she was about to condemn him for donating to children's hospitals. The reversal makes Lucille look monstrous (which she is) and Michael's deadpan completion is perfect.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure. Lucille's animosity toward charity is fully in character, and Michael completing her accusation as compliment is a beautiful inversion. The comedy comes entirely from character logic — Lucille genuinely would resent someone for publicly donating to children. High craft.

jokes.text: Nonetheless, we could get a giant checkbook too. We're just not that starved for attention.
jokes.setup: Michael has just pointed out that Sitwell waves checks for children's hospitals.
jokes.punchline: Lucille suggests they could do the same but frames it as beneath them — then immediately reveals deep insecurity by explaining they simply choose NOT to seek attention, which is itself an attention-seeking statement.
jokes.explanation: Good follow-through on the Lucille character beat. Her self-contradicting claim to not be 'starved for attention' is classic Lucille obliviousness, though it's a slightly softer landing than the previous joke.

jokes.text: Sorry that my son couldn't be here — Let me guess. Girlfriend. — You know, it could be worse. He could wanna marry your mother. [laughs] — Oh, I'm sorry. Is your family not laughing at that yet?
jokes.setup: Michael apologizes that George Michael isn't there, Sitwell jokes about his son wanting to marry 'your mother.'
jokes.punchline: Sitwell laughs at his own joke, then realizes no one else is laughing and awkwardly asks if the family hasn't gotten to a point where they can joke about it yet — implying this is a known scandal. The self-awareness makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: A cringe comedy gem. Sitwell's joke about George Michael potentially marrying Lucille (referencing the George Sr./Lucille dynamic, or possibly Buster's Oedipal attachment) lands wrong, and his self-conscious recognition ('Is your family not laughing at that yet?') makes it perfectly uncomfortable. The specificity of 'yet' implies it's just a matter of time before it's funny — darkly insightful.

jokes.text: You know, Sudden Valley conjures up the image of a sinkhole, no? — I told that to Dad. — I'm thinking — Paradise Gardens. [wind whistles] — I swear to God, I have that written—
jokes.setup: Sitwell suggests the development name 'Sudden Valley' is off-putting and proposes 'Paradise Gardens.'
jokes.punchline: Michael has clearly proposed 'Paradise Gardens' to his father before and been shot down. His frantic scramble to show he had it written down — proving his ideas are stolen by others — is both funny and sad. The wind whistling as he opens his notebook is an implied visual joke about how dramatically he pulls it out.
jokes.explanation: Good comedic structure — sets up the pattern of Michael's ideas being dismissed then vindicated. The rewatch bonus comes from understanding how consistently George Sr. has suppressed Michael's competence. 'I swear to God, I have that written—' is a wonderful interrupted frantic moment.

jokes.text: Sudden Valley just sort of implies that something awful could happen all of a sudden. — Plus, it's on a hill. — What are you taking, stupid pills? Come on. Save us some money.
jokes.setup: Flashback: Michael suggests to George Sr. that 'Sudden Valley' is a bad name.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. dismisses a perfectly sensible observation with 'What are you taking, stupid pills?' — the narrator establishes this is a management tool to keep Michael seeking approval. The joke is that Michael's objection is completely correct.
jokes.explanation: The flashback efficiently establishes George Sr.'s psychological manipulation of Michael. 'Stupid pills' is a memorably ridiculous insult. The narrator's analysis of the management tool makes the darkness explicit, which is very AD.

jokes.text: The only thing I ask is out of the 450 homes we build, one be given to a disadvantaged family from the inner city. — That's great. So the other 449 families live in fear? Is that what we're saying? I mean, come on! Where's your decen—
jokes.setup: Sitwell asks that one home out of 450 go to a disadvantaged family.
jokes.punchline: Gob's objection — that the other 449 families will 'live in fear' — is both absurdly racist/classist and so perfectly Gob that it functions as character comedy. He interrupts his own rant with 'Where's your decen—' implying he was about to say 'decency' while demonstrating none.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Gob moment. The joke works on multiple levels: it's satire of housing discrimination, perfect character comedy (Gob as performative moralist with zero self-awareness), and the interrupted 'decency' is an inspired touch — he was about to invoke decency while saying something indecent. Very high character integration.

jokes.text: What the hell just fell off your face? — One of this guy's eyebrows fell in the candy beans. — I always carry a spare.
jokes.setup: Mid-meeting, Sitwell's prosthetic eyebrow (due to alopecia) falls off into the candy beans.
jokes.punchline: Sitwell matter-of-factly notes 'I always carry a spare' — he's so accustomed to this happening that he has a system. The mundane practicality of the spare eyebrow makes it funnier than any dramatic reaction would.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional physical comedy that works entirely through dialogue. 'I always carry a spare' is a perfectly calibrated response — the deadpan dignity of a man who has adapted completely to his condition landing in a bowl of candy beans. This is also setup for the candy beans running gag and the eyebrow-in-the-beans visual is inherently funny. The specificity (candy beans, not just 'a bowl of food') adds texture. Comfortably an 8+.

jokes.text: Well, I hope you also carry a spare bowl of candy beans.
jokes.setup: Sitwell says he always carries a spare eyebrow.
jokes.punchline: Gob points out that the eyebrow contaminated the candy beans — so Sitwell needs a spare bowl too. The escalation of the problem (not just the eyebrow but the bowl it fell into) is petty and perfect.
jokes.explanation: Good callback. Gob's fussiness about the candy beans — a running show prop — is in character (petty, specific). The line is economical and lands as a secondary laugh off the main gag.

jokes.text: Pretty good? It was horrible. What are you taking, stupid pills again?
jokes.setup: Michael reports upstairs that Sitwell's offer sounds 'pretty good.' George Sr. dismisses it.
jokes.punchline: 'What are you taking, stupid pills again?' — the callback to the earlier flashback, now used live. The word 'again' implies this is a habitual exchange, and the narrative has just explained it's a control mechanism.
jokes.explanation: The callback earns a rewatch bonus because the audience has just been told this is a deliberate control tactic, so seeing it deployed immediately is both funny and slightly chilling. The comedy is now dual-layered: joke + character insight.

jokes.text: While Stan Sitwell used the game to raise funds for underprivileged children. — Oh, I think one of your orphans is heading towards the street. — Strike three.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Sitwell uses the softball game for charity. George Sr. uses it to compete ruthlessly.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. distracts Sitwell with a fake alert about an orphan in danger — pure psychological warfare. 'Strike three' from the narrator confirms it worked. The casual weaponization of orphan-concern is darkly perfect.
jokes.explanation: George Sr. distracting Sitwell with false concern for his charity orphans to gain a competitive advantage is a brilliant dark-comedy beat. 'Strike three' as the narrator's deadpan scorekeeping is perfectly economical. High character integration — only George Sr. would stoop this low with this much glee.

jokes.text: But you were right. It was a bad offer and you were smart enough not to take it. You got gumption. You speak your mind. That means you're good enough to work for me. — [bleep] you. — And I accepted.
jokes.setup: Sitwell compliments Gob's directness in telling him off.
jokes.punchline: Gob responds to further compliments with another '[bleep] you' — and then immediately reveals he accepted the job offer. The joke is that Gob's obscenity is both defiant AND his job interview, and he doesn't see the contradiction.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed reversal. Gob insulting someone IS the quality that gets him hired, and his immediate acceptance after one more 'bleep you' shows he has no coherent self-awareness — he's simultaneously defiant and completely compliant. The structure is clean: insult → hired → insult → hired. An 8 because it's smart about character while also being a big laugh.

jokes.text: We're gonna swing by his wigmaker. His 'just woke up' hair is finally ready.
jokes.setup: Gob announces he's joining Sitwell and they're making a stop.
jokes.punchline: Sitwell's 'just woke up' hair — a custom wig for casual morning appearance — is an absurdly specific detail. The idea that someone with alopecia would have a naturalistic 'bedhead' wig commissioned reveals both Sitwell's vanity and an entire world of wig culture existing offscreen.
jokes.explanation: Delightful world-building joke. The specificity of 'just woke up hair' as a distinct wig type is inherently funny — Sitwell is vain in unexpected ways for someone with alopecia. Small but well-crafted.

jokes.text: Well, here we are and home we go, with plenty of time to get you to English class.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Maeby have 'mapped the route' to the audition, arriving at the studio gate.
jokes.punchline: Tobias immediately pivots to going home — he did the preparation (drove to the studio) but his confidence failed completely before he even tried to get in. His chipper 'plenty of time to get you to English class' is both sad and absurd.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly Tobias beat — grand theatrical gesture followed by immediate capitulation. His cheerfulness about giving up is the funniest part. High character integration.

jokes.text: [At the studio gate] — Damn it! — Dad, you're auditioning for the part of a confidence man. Let's try a little confidence.
jokes.setup: Tobias's attempt at confidence at the studio gate fails immediately.
jokes.punchline: Maeby points out that he is literally auditioning to play a confidence man while demonstrating zero confidence — the dramatic irony is explicit and Maeby gets to be the wiser character.
jokes.explanation: The irony is clean and the role reversal (teenage daughter coaching helpless adult father) is a recurring Tobias/Maeby dynamic that works reliably.

jokes.text: Tobias says he's intimidated — 'behind these walls are the biggest stars in the world. America's treasures.' Cut to: Carl Weathers in the studio, clearly not an 'America's treasure' in the same league Tobias imagines.
jokes.setup: Tobias rhapsodizes about the stars inside the studio gates.
jokes.punchline: The cut reveals 'America's treasures' — a joke about the disparity between Tobias's awed reverence and the actual B-list reality of the studio. (Carl Weathers appears to be the 'treasure.')
jokes.explanation: Good visual juxtaposition joke. The narrator's 'America's treasures' cut is a classic AD technique — setting up an expectation and then revealing a comic deflation.

jokes.text: You say confidence man? Hey, I'm up for that part too. — Oh. — Good luck to you. — Good luck to you too.
jokes.setup: Maeby tells Tobias to try confidence; an actor nearby overhears 'confidence man' and reveals he's also auditioning.
jokes.punchline: The two actors exchange genuinely cordial 'good luck to you' pleasantries — which is mundane until you realize they're competing for the same role as con men and can't even muster competitive edge. The cheerfulness is absurdly wholesome.
jokes.explanation: A mild absurdist beat. The mutual politeness of competing actors auditioning for con men is gently funny but not a standout. Works as a transitional beat.

jokes.text: What do you think of when you hear the words 'Sudden Valley'? — Salad dressing, I think. But for some reason, I don't wanna eat it. — Right. But 'Paradise Gardens.' — Yeah. Okay. I can see marinating a chicken in that.
jokes.setup: Michael asks George Michael what 'Sudden Valley' sounds like to test his naming instinct.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'salad dressing I don't wanna eat' is a brilliant intuitive answer — he's unconsciously identifying the name's problem (something you avoid). 'I can see marinating a chicken in that' for Paradise Gardens is the perfect absurdist follow-up.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. George Michael accidentally delivers the most cogent market research of the episode through completely naive food-association. 'Salad dressing I don't wanna eat' is a strikingly original way to describe a bad brand name — it has the right category (food/fresh/valley) but something fundamentally off-putting about it. 'Marinating a chicken' for Paradise Gardens is beautifully absurd. The joke also characterizes George Michael as instinctively smart without knowing it. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Narrator: The league requirement for female players was 'difficult to meet given Gob's behavior in the third inning of last year's game.' Cut to Gob hitting on female players from opposing team during the game itself.
jokes.setup: Michael needs female players for the softball team.
jokes.punchline: The reason they're hard to recruit is shown in a quick montage of Gob hitting on or harassing female players — mid-game — losing court cases as a result.
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure — third inning, fifth inning, before the game — with 'court cases' as the kicker is very well-paced. 'Lost our entire outfield and a couple of court cases' is an excellent payoff line.

jokes.text: We lost our entire outfield and a couple of court cases.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains why it's hard to find female players, referencing Gob's behavior in previous years' softball games.
jokes.punchline: Losing the outfield is bad enough; the casual addition of 'a couple of court cases' escalates the consequence to absurd proportions. The narrator's flat delivery makes it darker.
jokes.explanation: The economy here is superb — a single subordinate clause converts a sports story into a legal disaster and makes Gob's behavior seem catastrophically routine. Very quotable one-liner from the narrator.

jokes.text: Well, you know who would be great and not at all litigious is Ann.
jokes.setup: Michael needs female players who won't sue from Gob's behavior. George Michael suggests Ann.
jokes.punchline: 'Not at all litigious' as a selling point for a softball recruit is hilariously practical — it also implies 'she would be too passive/meek to sue even if something bad happened.'
jokes.explanation: Smart observation that works on two levels — practical (she won't sue) and characterological (Ann is so unassuming she'd accept anything). 'Not at all litigious' is a funny word choice for a teenager talking about his girlfriend.

jokes.text: 'Cause you know this game is kind of our thing. But Ann's amazing. You know, she could play my position. — You should be playing the field... there, in right, you know?
jokes.setup: George Michael says Ann could play his position. Michael suggests George Michael should 'play the field.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'you should be playing the field' is a double entendre — literally (baseball outfield) and figuratively (dating around, not being monogamous with Ann). His hasty addition of 'there, in right, you know' shows he's conscious of the double meaning and awkwardly walking it back.
jokes.explanation: A well-deployed double entendre with the added layer of Michael catching himself and trying to specify which meaning he meant. The backpedal makes it more honest and funnier than a clean pun would be.

jokes.text: I got Aunt Lindsay playing this year. I can't very well pull her off the roster. — Let's make Ann the backup, okay? Very good way to think about her... as a backup.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to minimize Ann's involvement while managing George Michael's expectations.
jokes.punchline: 'Very good way to think about her... as a backup' — Michael turning a dismissal of Ann into gentle life coaching for George Michael. He's simultaneously telling his son how to think about his relationship and justifying softball logistics.
jokes.explanation: Excellent dual-register joke. Michael is giving George Michael relationship advice disguised as softball roster management. 'Very good way to think about her... as a backup' is a slightly cruel but entirely Michael thing to say.

jokes.text: Maybe — Maybe now I'll get a kiss. — Okay. — Oh. I meant from Ann. — Yes, I know. I just want to get in there first. [quietly] Okay.
jokes.setup: George Michael is excited that Ann is joining the softball team and mentions wanting a kiss.
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'okay' — implying he wants a kiss from his son — then both immediately clarify ('I meant from Ann' / 'I just want to get in there first') in the most uncomfortable father-son exchange possible. The 'quietly' stage direction suggests a deeply awkward beat.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The accidental implication of Michael wanting to kiss his own son, followed by BOTH of them awkwardly clarifying in sequence — with Michael saying 'I just want to get in there first' which is ALSO weird — is a perfect cringe escalation. The quiet 'Okay' at the end is devastating. High originality, perfect structure, very character-specific to Michael's complicated feelings about George Michael's romantic life.

jokes.text: Lindsay, wake up. I need you to play on the softball team. — [Narrator:] Lindsay felt the intended effect of Teamocil — a sense of camaraderie. — Count me in, Michael. Happy to help the team.
jokes.setup: Michael wakes Lindsay to ask her to play softball. She's on Teamocil.
jokes.punchline: The narrator explicitly identifies that her cheerful 'Count me in, Michael. Happy to help the team' is the Teamocil working — she's agreeable not because she wants to help but because the drug has induced artificial camaraderie. The word 'intended' is key — even the intended effect is pharmacological.
jokes.explanation: A good structural joke — the narrator's label makes Lindsay's sincerity darkly ironic. The drug making her a better team player is both funny and a commentary on the family's fundamental inability to cooperate naturally.

jokes.text: Yeah, I don't think things are gonna work out with Sitwell. I mean, we picked up his sleepy-head wig, everything was cool. Next thing I know, he's asking me for a bunch of work ideas. So, I thought I'd just quit and go back to being president here.
jokes.setup: Gob returns almost immediately after taking the Sitwell job.
jokes.punchline: His entire tenure lasted long enough to pick up a wig, and he quit the moment actual work was expected. He recounts this as if 'he asked me for work ideas' is an unreasonable demand. The non-sequitur of 'sleepy-head wig' followed by the immediate job abandonment is very Gob.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the wig while encapsulating Gob's entire work ethic (none) is efficiently done. His matter-of-fact description of quitting because someone asked him to work is peak character comedy.

jokes.text: Michael, I'm your big brother. I'll never be impressed with you.
jokes.setup: Michael says he needs to prove he can do the job — 'to you.' Gob responds.
jokes.punchline: Gob's flat declaration that he will NEVER be impressed with Michael — stated as simple fact, not insult — is both brutally honest and a perfect character moment. He's not being cruel; he genuinely thinks this is neutral information.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The bluntness is total — Gob isn't trying to hurt Michael, he's just stating what he considers fact — which makes it both funny and devastating. The line has perfect economy (nine words that define their entire relationship), exceptional character integration (only Gob delivers this with zero malice), and a dark undertone about brotherly dynamics. This is legitimately in 8+ territory: it's quotable, insightful, and darkly true.

jokes.text: I just want the office and my stuffs and title.
jokes.setup: Gob says Michael can still do the actual president job.
jokes.punchline: 'My stuffs' — the childish plural of 'stuff' reveals Gob's actual priorities: his possessions and title, not any responsibility. The word choice is small but characterful.
jokes.explanation: A small character-language joke. 'Stuffs' is a deliberately childish word choice that suits Gob's arrested development (no pun intended). Not a standout but contributes to the scene's texture.

jokes.text: What about if I help you with your work over at Sitwell? — You'd do that? — Sure. You really want me to succeed at Sitwell. — Well, I just — Hey. Come on. I really want you to be over at Sitwell.
jokes.setup: Michael offers to help Gob with his work at Sitwell. Gob is touched.
jokes.punchline: Michael blunders into revealing his actual motive — he wants Gob 'to be over at Sitwell,' i.e., away from the Bluth Company and out of Michael's way. Gob misreads brotherly support; Michael is actually just strategic. The correction from 'succeed at Sitwell' to 'be over at Sitwell' is a perfect slip.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The structural bait-and-switch ('succeed' → 'be') is clear, earned, and character-true. Michael's self-interest masquerading as brotherly support — and then being caught mid-sentence — is a defining Bluth family comedy beat. The long pause before 'I just — Hey. Come on' sells his attempt to cover. This is an 8.

jokes.text: I feel that we've not brought something up that might be a solution — um — to spare my feelings. — Send you in his place? I called. They don't want you back.
jokes.setup: Oscar hints that there's an obvious solution no one is mentioning, seemingly to protect him.
jokes.punchline: Lucille instantly knows he means 'send Oscar in Buster's place' and immediately reveals she already called to check — and the army rejected him. The speed of her comprehension and prior action is both funny and devastating.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's pre-emptive rejection of Oscar — she called ahead — is an efficient, brutal joke. The compassionate framing ('to spare my feelings') makes her actual contempt funnier.

jokes.text: [Vietnam flashback] Croc! No, sorry. Wait. Log. Just a log. No, wait. Sorry. Croc! — Oscar served as a croc spotter on a Swift boat.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Oscar served in Vietnam as a croc spotter.
jokes.punchline: His croc-spotting is depicted as hilariously unreliable — 'Croc! Log. No, wait. Croc!' — suggesting his entire military service was a series of false alarms. 'Croc spotter' is also an absurd military role.
jokes.explanation: An absurdist military flashback that works through specificity — 'croc spotter' as a ridiculous naval role, and Oscar's incompetence at it captured in a rapid staccato of false identifications. Good economy of the bit.

jokes.text: [Young Lucille] I prefer an older man. — [Bob Hope] Hey, I'm free after the show. — I said older, Bob, not dead.
jokes.setup: Young Lucille meets Bob Hope at a USO show and says she prefers older men.
jokes.punchline: When Bob Hope offers himself, she says 'older, not dead' — insulting Bob Hope's age while also establishing Lucille's lifelong taste for powerful older men (which explains everything about her marriage to George Sr. and her relationship to authority).
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed celebrity cameo joke — Bob Hope is invoked not just as a name-drop but as the target of Lucille's withering wit. 'Older, not dead' is highly quotable and characterizes both the young Lucille (sharp, predatory) and the elder Bob Hope (punchline to his own legend). Good economy and character integration.

jokes.text: That Fünke is some kind of something. — Boy, this Fünke is all anybody's ever talking about. — So sick and tired of hearing about how brilliant that Fünke is. — Overrated. I'll just move — I'm sorry. — Okay. We're okay.
jokes.setup: Tobias has talked up 'the name Fünke' around the studio watercooler.
jokes.punchline: All the praise is coming from the same person (Tobias) talking to himself at different spots along the watercooler — culminating in him walking into something mid-self-hype and recovering. The escalating self-promotion to an audience of one is delusional but earnest.
jokes.explanation: A standout set piece. Tobias literally performing all his own PR buzz — voicing multiple fake admirers around a watercooler, each one more effusive, then walking into something — is a brilliant absurdist construction that only works for Tobias. The escalation ('some kind of something' → 'all anybody's ever talking about' → 'so sick and tired of hearing') mirrors real hype cycles but with a cast of one. High originality and character integration. The physical interruption ('I'll just move — I'm sorry') is the perfect cap. This is an 8+ because the concept is genuinely clever and the execution is tight.

jokes.text: Sorry, is this your office? — No. I'm just sitting behind someone else's desk pretending these are my kids.
jokes.setup: Maeby enters what appears to be an occupied office and apologizes.
jokes.punchline: The executive's sarcastic 'I'm just sitting behind someone else's desk pretending these are my kids' is a surprisingly bleak self-revelation — or perfect dry sarcasm — implying Hollywood executives are all imposters with fake family photos.
jokes.explanation: A sharp one-liner that works both as deadpan sarcasm and as potential actual truth about the entertainment industry. The ambiguity (is he joking? is this really not his office?) adds texture.

jokes.text: Can you get Jude Law in here for a meeting? — Oh, I'm just a reader. — A reader? — Yeah. — I need a report by tomorrow. Before gym-lunch... with Jim... Carrey.
jokes.setup: Maeby (a teenager) asks someone to get Jude Law in a meeting. The person says they're just a reader. Maeby immediately gives them an assignment.
jokes.punchline: Maeby, improvising a Hollywood executive persona, assigns the reader a report — then name-drops 'Jim Carrey' with deliberate hesitation to make it sound casual. 'Gym-lunch with Jim Carrey' is an absurd specific detail that sells the bluff.
jokes.explanation: Maeby's instant pivot from being caught to asserting executive authority is in perfect character. The hesitation on 'Jim... Carrey' — performed casually to signal she does this all the time — is a great physical comedy beat (inferred from text). 'The Grinch guy. You're too young' adds a layer of humor about Carrey's career.

jokes.text: It's the Grinch guy. You're too young.
jokes.setup: Maeby hesitantly name-drops Jim Carrey as if she might be misremembering him.
jokes.punchline: The reader helpfully clarifies which Jim Carrey film by saying 'It's the Grinch guy. You're too young' — which is both accurate and condescending, while also subtly calling out that Maeby is clearly a child playing executive.
jokes.explanation: A decent follow-beat. The characterization of Carrey as 'the Grinch guy' is slightly unflattering (given his range) and the 'you're too young' is an appropriate light callback. Not exceptional.

jokes.text: So get this. Sitwell loved my ideas. — Well, my ideas and I knew it. That's great. — You know, Dad never complimented any of those. — Here's the thing. I need more. — There were 34 proposals in there.
jokes.setup: Gob reports that Sitwell loved 'his' ideas — which are actually Michael's.
jokes.punchline: Michael's resigned '...well, my ideas' clarification followed by 'I need more' — Gob has burned through 34 of Michael's proposals in one sitting and needs more content to pass off as his own.
jokes.explanation: Good family dynamic comedy — Gob is stealing Michael's intellectual labor with zero self-awareness, and Michael's correction is so tired and practiced it's barely even angry. The '34 proposals' detail makes the scale of the theft absurdly large.

jokes.text: You'd be amazed how fast they come out when you read them all in a row. 'Thirty-one, bamboo floor upgrades. Cheaper, stronger.' I can't read this word. 'Thirty-two, wireless crapability.' That one explains itself.
jokes.setup: Gob reads Michael's proposals to him, apparently in order, having already used 30 of them.
jokes.punchline: 'Wireless crapability' — whether a typo for 'capability' or a deliberate Michael neologism — and Gob's deadpan 'that one explains itself' is perfect. Also Gob can't read one word of Michael's handwriting, suggesting the proposals were never meant to be read aloud.
jokes.explanation: 'Wireless crapability' is an inspired bit of wordplay — ambiguous enough to be either a typo or an actual product feature. 'That one explains itself' is perfectly deadpan Gob, delivered with absolute confidence in something he doesn't understand.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Gob's own father had only once played catch with him. And it ended as badly as any game of catch could end.
jokes.setup: Sitwell buys Gob a ball and suggests they play catch — a fatherly gesture.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the only time George Sr. played catch with Gob 'ended as badly as any game of catch could end' — implying a traumatic event never specified. The absolute worst possible game of catch, presented as information and then abandoned. The unspecified horror is funnier than any specific would be.
jokes.explanation: Excellent use of the narrator. 'As badly as any game of catch could end' is maximally vague in a way that maximizes audience imagination — did someone lose an eye? get struck by lightning? The refusal to specify is the joke, and it's genuinely dark character insight: George Sr.'s parenting somehow turned a child's game into a worst-case scenario. High economy. Rewatch bonus because it retroactively reframes Gob's emotional hunger throughout the episode.

jokes.text: White power! — Better yet, maybe we can play ball on Saturday.
jokes.setup: The flashback of catch going wrong — someone shouts 'White power!' in the midst of whatever disaster unfolded.
jokes.punchline: The 'White power!' is presumably shouted in context of the catch (the ball? a flag? unclear) but sounds horrifically like a racist slogan. Sitwell's pivot to 'maybe we can play ball on Saturday' is immediate and desperate to move past whatever just happened.
jokes.explanation: The 'White power!' is a deliberately shocking non-sequitur that could mean anything in context — a white kite, a white ball, or exactly what it sounds like, with George Sr. — and the show deliberately refuses to clarify. Dark and subversive in a way typical of Arrested Development. The transition to softball invitation is perfect comedic whiplash.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Gob was both angry... and overwhelmed... by the approval from this new father figure. It was confusing.
jokes.setup: Sitwell has praised Gob and expressed fatherly warmth.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry analysis of Gob's emotional state ('It was confusing') following a beat of presumably visible conflict on Gob's face is perfectly deployed — the understatement ('confusing') for a man clearly having a breakdown of paternal longing is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's minimizing of Gob's obvious emotional turmoil ('It was confusing') is classic AD narrator comedy — using clinical understatement to describe profound dysfunction. Rewatch bonus because it sets up the episode's emotional climax.

jokes.text: [Softball flashback] Gob especially gifted at sacrificing his body — Play's at second! Play's at second! — Yeah! Suck on that, Sitwell! — Your Tweety Bird dance just cost us a run, you moron.
jokes.setup: George Sr. says Gob was the best softball player in the league.
jokes.punchline: The flashback shows Gob celebrating after a good play with a 'Tweety Bird dance' that distracts him enough to cost them a run — he's simultaneously the best and most self-sabotaging player. George Sr.'s 'you moron' in the same flashback where he's supposedly Gob's greatest champion is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The 'Tweety Bird dance' is an inspired specific detail — why Tweety Bird? — and George Sr. immediately calling him a moron in his own flashback of Gob's athletic greatness nicely undercuts his argument.

jokes.text: Lindsay, did that oven vent hit you? Your foot is bleeding. — My foot is bleeding. — [Narrator] Teamocil may cause numbness of the extremities.
jokes.setup: The oven vent (the running physical gag) falls and injures Lindsay.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay calmly repeats 'my foot is bleeding' as if processing information rather than experiencing pain. The narrator then reveals Teamocil causes numbness of the extremities — she literally cannot feel that she's been hurt.
jokes.explanation: A multi-callback payoff: the oven vent gag and the Teamocil side effects both pay off simultaneously. Lindsay's flat restatement of the injury as information is a perfectly calibrated deadpan. The narrator's timing is impeccable. This is a well-earned joke.

jokes.text: You're gonna be able to play on our team, right? — Why do you think I'm taking Teamocil? — To curb your sex drive. — That's right. What team are you talking about? — [Narrator] It may also cause short-term memory loss.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Lindsay can still play softball. She deflects. He correctly identifies the sex-drive angle. She agrees then reveals she's forgotten the conversation.
jokes.punchline: 'What team are you talking about?' — the narrator reveals this is Teamocil short-term memory loss. The side effects keep accumulating: no sex drive, numb extremities, now amnesia. Each revelation escalates the absurdity of Lindsay's self-medication.
jokes.explanation: The Teamocil side-effects escalation reaches its comedic peak here. Three side effects revealed across the episode, each more alarming than the last, and Lindsay cheerfully experiencing all of them. The structure is superb — each callback builds on the previous. The narrator's clinical delivery ('It may also cause short-term memory loss') as a postscript to Lindsay forgetting the question is excellent timing.

jokes.text: Okay. Looks like I'm gonna need that female backup player after all. — And Ann's really good at softball, huh? — Yeah. She's amazing. You know, she's got this low center of gravity. You can't knock her over. — Well, I could knock her over. — Dad, I'm telling you, you can't. — They call her 'The Wall,' you know? — Well, that's great. But I could knock her over. I won't, but I could.
jokes.setup: Michael needs Ann as backup. George Michael defends her softball abilities.
jokes.punchline: Michael's increasingly petulant insistence that he COULD knock over 'The Wall' ('I won't, but I could') is a perfect escalation — a grown man competing with his son's girlfriend's athletic reputation in the pettiest possible way.
jokes.explanation: Excellent. Michael's fixation on being able to knock Ann over ('I could. I won't, but I could') is both petty and deeply character-specific — Michael needs to be better than everyone around him even when it means competing with his son's girlfriend. 'They call her The Wall' is a perfect Ann Veal joke (she is, indeed, a wall of personality). The callback-escalation within a single exchange is clean.

jokes.text: Is there anything I can do? — Maybe if you... go downtown.
jokes.setup: Lucille is trying to get Buster out of deployment. The general says there's nothing he can do. She asks if there's anything SHE can do.
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe if you... go downtown' — a sexual euphemism for a sex act, delivered by the general as if it's a matter-of-fact negotiation. The ellipsis in the transcript implies a pause that makes it unmistakable while technically deniable.
jokes.explanation: A well-placed adult joke that works through implication and timing. The pause before 'go downtown' does the heavy lifting. In context (Lucille doing anything for her son, a general she once dated) it's appropriately dark and earns its place.

jokes.text: [Exhales] Oh, I haven't done that in 30 years.
jokes.setup: The general suggests Lucille 'go downtown.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's resigned, experienced sigh — 'I haven't done that in 30 years' — confirms she knows exactly what he means and has a specific timeline for when she last did it. The 30-year figure is the precise comic detail that makes it.
jokes.explanation: The punchline to the previous setup, and the stronger of the two jokes. The 30-year number implies she stopped around the time she married George Sr. (or possibly when the Bluth empire started). The exhausted resignation with which Lucille accepts this transaction is completely in character — she will do what she must, she just hasn't had to in decades. Very quotable.

jokes.text: You make me want to puke, Bluth! [giggling]
jokes.setup: Scene transition after the 'go downtown' moment — we cut to...
jokes.punchline: The general's 'You make me want to puke, Bluth!' accompanied by Lucille's giggling suggests we've cut back to them in an intimate moment — the exclamation is presumably affectionate in context, and Lucille's giggling is deeply unsettling.
jokes.explanation: A scene cut that implies a graphic act through implication alone — we don't see anything, but the general's 'you make me want to puke' followed by Lucille's giggling tells us everything. The cut structure is elegant cringe comedy.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And so, Buster was pulled out of combat training and put into U.S.O. training.
jokes.setup: Lucille arranged something with the general to save Buster.
jokes.punchline: The payoff: Buster goes from combat training to USO training — dancing and performing, not fighting. He's been saved from war by his mother's sexual favor and now has to do the exact same job young Lucille was doing when she met the general. The circularity is darkly comic.
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff is elegant: Lucille was a USO dancer who met the general; now her son becomes a USO performer because of that same general. The narrator's flat delivery of 'USO training' lands the punch. Rewatch bonus for anyone who caught the callback.

jokes.text: It jolts, but it's not really doing it right now.
jokes.setup: Buster is apparently practicing some USO dance move or prop.
jokes.punchline: The line 'It jolts, but it's not really doing it right now' is an oblique description of a prop that's not working properly — but taken out of context sounds deeply wrong. The matter-of-fact delivery about something 'jolting' is very Buster.
jokes.explanation: A weaker joke that relies on implied visual context we can't fully access in the transcript. Works slightly better on screen. Scored lower because the humor doesn't land as well in text form and seems transitional.

jokes.text: Nobody makes a fool of our family without my help.
jokes.setup: Gob agrees to throw the softball game to protect the family from being made fools by Sitwell.
jokes.punchline: 'Nobody makes a fool of our family without my help' — Gob acknowledges that HE is the primary fool-maker of the family while framing it as a territorial defense. He's not saying he won't make them look foolish; he's saying Sitwell can't do it without his cooperation.
jokes.explanation: The best line in the episode and one of the great sitcom lines in the series. The joke works on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) literal — Gob is agreeing to help throw the game (help Sitwell make fools of them), (2) ironic — he's so oblivious he doesn't realize he's the main source of family humiliation, (3) character — it perfectly captures Gob's delusion that he's the family's protector, (4) structural — the phrase 'without my help' does everything, turning a defiant statement into a self-own in three words. The economy is near-perfect (nine words, total story of Gob Bluth). This is a legitimate 9+ — it's instantly quotable, character-defining, structurally brilliant, and earns every repeat viewing.

jokes.text: What's your name? — Tobias. — He's too short. Give it to the guard.
jokes.setup: Tobias finally gets to the audition. He says his name.
jokes.punchline: The casting director immediately rejects him not for any performance reason but simply for height — before Tobias can say anything else. The transaction takes about three seconds. Tobias's preparation, his whole journey, is ended instantly for a reason he can't control.
jokes.explanation: The speed of Tobias's rejection after all his preparation is the joke — the whole arc deflated in one sentence. 'Give it to the guard' as the dismissal is efficiently cruel. High economy.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm not. I — on camera, I seem much taller.
jokes.setup: Casting director says Tobias is too short.
jokes.punchline: 'On camera, I seem much taller' — Tobias's desperate claim is the opposite of the photographic truth (cameras typically make people look shorter/wider). The earnestness of the objection makes it sadder and funnier.
jokes.explanation: A decent Tobias desperation beat. The claim is factually wrong (wide-angle lenses make people shorter) but Tobias's total belief in it is sad and characteristic. Mid-tier joke.

jokes.text: You're the Fünke everyone's talking about? What are you, like, 15? — Marry me. [chuckles] — Everyone thinks I look young too.
jokes.setup: The film executive discovers 'Fünke' (who's been creating massive buzz) is apparently a 15-year-old girl.
jokes.punchline: His instant 'Marry me' — as a compliment to her business acumen — is immediately unsettling given her apparent age. Maeby's 'Everyone thinks I look young too' is a perfect mirror of Tobias's 'I seem much taller on camera' — both Fünkes claim they're not what they appear.
jokes.explanation: The parallel with Tobias's scene is elegant — both Fünkes are asked about their apparent wrongness (too short, too young) and both deflect with variations of 'I seem differently than I appear.' Maeby's accidental industry success contrasted with Tobias's immediate failure is the episode's funniest structural joke. The 'Marry me' is creepy-funny in the right register.

jokes.text: Jude Law. Why do you ask? — Yeah. Go young. Young Guy and the Sea. Big spring-breaker. Four C.G.I. the fish. Let's fast-track this one.
jokes.setup: Maeby's book report (which was actually a script reader's report) suggests Jude Law for the lead.
jokes.punchline: The executive's instant green-light — renaming 'The Old Man and the Sea' as 'Young Guy and the Sea,' making it a 'spring-breaker,' CGI-ing the fish — is a perfect satire of Hollywood development. Four words ('Four C.G.I. the fish') encapsulate an entire industry pathology.
jokes.explanation: A sharp Hollywood satire compressed into three sentences. 'Young Guy and the Sea' as an instant Hemingway adaptation that strips all meaning, 'big spring-breaker,' and 'four CGI the fish' as executive shorthand are all individually funny and collectively devastating about the film industry. Economy is excellent — this could have been five minutes of commentary but lands in ten words.

jokes.text: It's all waxed up. / All waxed up? What do you mean? / I need something. A job for my dad. / Yeah, yeah. Sure. Okay. / Listen to me. Brand new cars don't get waxed!
jokes.setup: Maeby is negotiating with the film executive as though she's a real executive
jokes.punchline: Maeby immediately leverages her new power to get her father a job; the executive agrees instantly — then pivots to screaming at someone about car wax
jokes.explanation: The Hollywood executive pivoting from business deals to screaming about car wax is a good character detail. Maeby's immediate use of her power to help her father (while setting up the 'next week' teaser) is good plot comedy.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And that's when Maeby realized she had become a successful film executive.
jokes.setup: Maeby has accidentally gotten a project greenlit and been given authority she doesn't have.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's flat acknowledgment — 'that's when Maeby realized she had become a successful film executive' — treats a 15-year-old's accidental career as a simple fact. The understatement is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's matter-of-fact treatment of Maeby's absurd situation is well deployed. The word 'realized' implies it caught even her off guard, which is funny. Sets up her ongoing plot arc.

jokes.text: It's all waxed up. — All waxed up? What do you mean? — I need something. A job for my dad. — Yeah, yeah. Sure. Okay. — Listen to me. Brand new cars don't get waxed!
jokes.setup: Maeby (now an accidental executive) negotiates a job for her father. The executive seems distracted by a car issue.
jokes.punchline: The executive's sudden explosion about waxing brand-new cars — completely unrelated to their negotiation — is a perfect absurdist non-sequitur that reveals Hollywood executives as chaotic, distracted people who agree to things (a job for Tobias) mid-crisis.
jokes.explanation: The wax crisis is an effectively absurd Hollywood-exec character beat. The agreement to hire Tobias happening in the background of an unrelated meltdown is funny because it confirms Hollywood works through chaos and inattention.

jokes.text: Spin, spin, spin. — [Narrator] Ann wasn't as amazing as George Michael led his father to believe.
jokes.setup: George Michael praised Ann's softball abilities effusively, building her up to 'The Wall' status.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan 'Ann wasn't as amazing as George Michael led his father to believe' — presumably over a shot of Ann badly missing a ball or spinning helplessly — is a perfect deflation of the entire George Michael/Ann setup.
jokes.explanation: The callback to George Michael's passionate defense of Ann pays off with a simple visual revelation. 'Spin, spin, spin' in the transcript implies an on-screen visual of Ann spinning helplessly — the gap between the promise ('The Wall') and the reality (spinning in place) is the perfect joke. The narrator's understated delivery is the bow.

jokes.text: Time out. — Gob, are you okay? — Yeah, just can't hit today. Guess you're really disappointed in me. — Hey. Everybody has days like this. It's only a game. — After that list of ideas you gave me, I don't care if you blow the whole season. — Come here, you.
jokes.setup: Gob is throwing the game. Sitwell shows fatherly concern.
jokes.punchline: Gob's response ('Guess you're really disappointed in me') reveals he's trying to tank the game but is simultaneously HOPING to disappoint a father figure, which Sitwell completely subverts by expressing total approval. Gob's plan is being defeated by genuine warmth.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully character-integrated moment. Gob is trying to sabotage the game while simultaneously craving paternal approval from the man he's sabotaging, and the sabotage is failing because Sitwell genuinely likes him. The irony operates on every level — the joke and the tragedy are the same thing.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And Gob finally felt that someone believed in him. Even though they weren't his ideas. And he decided to make Sitwell proud.
jokes.setup: Sitwell praises Gob for Michael's ideas and shows him warmth.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'Even though they weren't his ideas' is the dark kicker — Gob is being validated for something he didn't do, and he's so starved for approval he doesn't care. It's simultaneously the funniest and saddest sentence in the episode.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's parenthetical 'Even though they weren't his ideas' does enormous work — it's the joke and the pathos simultaneously. Gob's emotional arc (anger → confusion → desperate approval-seeking → genuine joy) is compressed into three sentences. The economy is exceptional and the character integration is complete.

jokes.text: You better pull an ankle or something. — Come on, George Michael! Get that ball in there! — [Narrator] Then he went headfirst into The Wall. — I'm safe. — You're out! — Out? I was safe! I'm safe!
jokes.setup: Gob has decided to play seriously to make Sitwell proud. Michael tells him to fake an injury.
jokes.punchline: Gob literally runs headfirst into Ann ('The Wall') — paying off her nickname established earlier — and argues he's safe. The nickname becomes literal physics. The argument ('I was safe! I'm safe!') while clearly injured is pure Gob.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. Ann being called 'The Wall' pays off literally when Gob runs into her headfirst. The joke works on multiple levels: (1) the nickname is redeemed (she DID stop someone), (2) Michael was right that you 'couldn't knock her over,' (3) Gob's continued insistence he's 'safe' after a head collision is perfect. The structure of setting up the nickname episodes earlier and paying it off here is clean Arrested Development plotting.

jokes.text: I'm safe. / You're out! / Out? I was safe! I'm safe!
jokes.setup: Gob goes headfirst into Ann and is called out
jokes.punchline: After his emotional journey, Gob's agreement to throw the game, and his physical sacrifice — he's called out anyway. His insistence he's safe is pure Gob ego refusing defeat
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Gob refusing to accept the umpire's call is consistent with his character. Comes immediately after a stronger joke so slightly diminished.

jokes.text: Stop with the Tweety Bird dance, you moron. — Dad? — He made the call. You're out. What are you doing? — Shush, shush, shush. — Dad? — He's safe. — What the hell are you doing here? — I'm taking care of what you screwed up. You blew it again, Michael.
jokes.setup: George Sr. appears at the softball game (despite being a fugitive hiding at home).
jokes.punchline: George Sr. has snuck out of the house to interfere in the softball game and immediately (1) references the Tweety Bird dance callback, (2) overrules the umpire, and (3) tells Michael he 'blew it again.' His entire purpose in escaping his hiding spot was to meddle in a softball game.
jokes.explanation: George Sr. emerging from hiding to interfere in a softball game is inherently funny because the stakes are so wrong. His immediate 'You blew it again, Michael' upon arrival crystallizes their entire relationship. The Tweety Bird callback lands because it's been used twice before.

jokes.text: Stop with the Tweety Bird dance, you moron. / Dad?
jokes.setup: Someone is apparently doing the Tweety Bird dance at the game
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is present at the game — something Michael didn't know — shouting about the Tweety Bird dance, which callbacks to the flashback earlier. Michael's 'Dad?' reveals he's shocked his fugitive father has left hiding to attend a softball game
jokes.explanation: The Tweety Bird dance callback landing at a moment of genuine surprise (George Sr. appearing at the game) is well-timed. Michael's 'Dad?' — one word conveying shock that a fugitive has left the house — is the strong element.

jokes.text: I even stooped to cheat here at a softball game. — You've been talking to each other. How come nobody called me?
jokes.setup: Michael confronts his father about the whole situation.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s complaint — 'How come nobody called me?' — reveals that his primary objection to Michael and Gob's conspiracy isn't that they conspired, but that he was excluded from it. The self-centered injured tone is perfect Bluth patriarch behavior.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the total self-absorption of George Sr.'s grievance — not 'you cheated' but 'why wasn't I included in the cheating?' Perfectly in character and delivers a big laugh through total moral obliviousness.

jokes.text: Michael confronts his father: 'I even stooped to cheat here at a softball game.' George Sr.: 'You've been talking to each other. How come nobody called me?'
jokes.setup: Michael confesses to cheating at the softball game — a moral crisis for him.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s response is not moral outrage but offended exclusion — his only concern is that he wasn't included in the cheating scheme.
jokes.explanation: 'How come nobody called me?' as George Sr.'s only concern about a cheating scheme perfectly encapsulates his character — amoral, self-centered, wanting to be included regardless of the ethics. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Yeah, and Stan Sitwell liked my ideas. He really believed in me. — My ideas. — Well.
jokes.setup: Gob feels good about Sitwell's approval.
jokes.punchline: Michael's quiet 'My ideas' — not even a full sentence, just two words — and Gob's non-response ('Well.') encapsulates the entire episode: Michael does all the work, gets none of the credit, and isn't even angry enough to fight about it.
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic joke in perfect miniature. 'My ideas. — Well.' Two words and a non-committal response that summarizes the entire Michael/Gob/George Sr. dynamic across the whole series. The economy here is exceptional — nine-season story in two words. Gob's 'Well' is funnier than any denial or argument would be because it's a shrug. High craft.

jokes.text: I'm proud of you. — Really? — Yeah. See ya.
jokes.setup: Michael declares independence from his father's approval.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately says 'I'm proud of you' — exactly what Michael has always wanted — and then immediately leaves. The approval Michael has spent his life seeking is delivered and withdrawn in four words. 'See ya' is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional ending joke. The 'I'm proud of you — Really? — Yeah. See ya.' structure compresses Michael's entire psychological arc into a six-word exchange. The approval comes the moment Michael says he doesn't need it (classic), it's real for about 0.5 seconds, then it's literally walked away from. 'See ya' is perfect — casual, dismissive, fatherly in the worst way. One of the episode's best moments. The economy is near-perfect and it's entirely character-integrated.

jokes.text: Michael, I could've sworn I just saw Dad. — Or not. I think hallucination is one of the side-effects of Teamocil. — [Narrator] It isn't.
jokes.setup: Gob thinks he saw their father, who is a fugitive and shouldn't be there.
jokes.punchline: Michael dismisses it as a Teamocil hallucination. The narrator immediately corrects: 'It isn't.' — meaning Gob really did see George Sr., and Michael is wrong, and the narrator is adding a new fake Teamocil side effect correction. The one-word correction is devastating.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest jokes in the episode. Three things happen simultaneously: (1) the Teamocil gag gets a final callback (hallucination is NOT a side effect — the running gag of listing real side effects gets punctured), (2) it reveals George Sr. was really there (Michael is wrong about what's real), (3) the narrator's one-word correction 'It isn't.' is maximally economical. The structure is near-perfect — setup (Gob's sighting) → false explanation (Teamocil) → authority correction (narrator). The 'It isn't' does enormous work. This is a legitimately great joke that earns the 8+ rating.

jokes.text: Sorry I didn't blow the game. — No. You did the right thing... and you finally got Dad to one of your ball games. — Screw him. — Yeah. [whistling through teeth] I miss him so much.
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob reflect on the game. Michael notes Gob 'finally got Dad to one of his ball games.'
jokes.punchline: Gob says 'Screw him' with conviction. Michael agrees. Then: '[whistling through teeth] I miss him so much.' The vulnerability — delivered presumably while looking away, the whistling signaling suppressed emotion — is both funny and devastating. It's the most honest Gob has been all episode.
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional close and one of its best jokes. The beat structure — defiance, agreement, then the muted '[whistling through teeth] I miss him so much' — is beautifully constructed. The stage direction 'whistling through teeth' implies Gob is trying NOT to say it but can't help himself. The whole episode has been about Gob's desperate need for paternal approval (from Sitwell), and the final line makes explicit what the episode has been about all along: he misses his actual father. The fact that it's played for comedy while being genuinely sad is the show at its best. Economy is exceptional — the contrast between 'screw him' and 'I miss him so much' separated only by 'Yeah.' is perfect.

jokes.text: [On the Next Arrested Development] Maeby gets her father a job. — I gotta fire one of ya. Andy Richter! Here are those head shots I was telling you about. — Yeah. I gotta go. — It's Tobias Fünke. I told you about the head shots?
jokes.setup: Maeby has arranged a job for Tobias at the studio. The executive needs to fire someone.
jokes.punchline: Andy Richter is about to be fired in favor of Tobias Fünke — a real celebrity being replaced by the hapless Tobias. Tobias then arrives with headshots and the executive has already forgotten Maeby's arrangement. The tag delivers a setup for the next episode.
jokes.explanation: The 'on the next' tag is a staple device. Andy Richter being cast as himself being replaced by Tobias is a good gag, and the executive's immediate amnesia about the deal pays off the wax-car bit from earlier. Functions as a teaser more than a standalone joke.

jokes.text: [Buster visits the general] I just wanted to thank you for my new job. [giggles] — What's wrong? — I'm shipping out tomorrow. — I've got just the thing. — Oh. I haven't felt hands like that in years. — You are coming to the front.
jokes.setup: Buster visits the general to thank him. The general reveals he's shipping out.
jokes.punchline: 'I haven't felt hands like that in years' — the general massages Buster's hands in what becomes increasingly Oedipal/sensual, and 'You are coming to the front' suggests a reversal of fortune. The hands joke references Buster's famous hand anxiety AND the general's own history with Lucille. The cringe escalates rapidly.
jokes.explanation: A dense callback: (1) the general touching Buster references Lucille touching the general, creating an Oedipal chain, (2) Buster's hands are a show-wide running gag about his hand fetish/fear, (3) the general sending Buster to the front reverses all of Lucille's efforts. 'I haven't felt hands like that in years' is creepily ambiguous — does he mean Lucille's hands? It's appropriately disturbing. Good episode-closing cringe.

tmdb_overview: The Bluths rival development company hires Gob, who finds the company president a father figure, in preparation for the Bluth-Sitwell softball game; Lindsay hallucinates from taking anti-depressants.
insight: Steady hand-off episode: 64 jokes in 29 minutes keeps pace without standout peaks.
